Transcrição Completa: Jeremy Haynes Branding

[01:00] For all those who don't know, Jersey milk that's produced by these cows actually produces, first of all, 18% more protein, 20% more calcium, and 25% more butter fat than the average cow's milk produces.

VISUAL: Lista de benefícios do produto, texto descritivo do site oficial, layout de e-commerce
[03:02] So to be clear, the benefits that they articulate to people is that it's made from Jersey cream, which a lot of people don't even know what that is, but it spikes your curiosity when you hear about it.
[03:12] Says it's churned butter with over 80% butter fat, a lot more than other types of butters. They don't even talk about the nutritional density of the product in comparison to other butters.
[03:22] They hand skim the butter fat off the top of the butter in comparison to using any type of machinery. That's a flex for them.
[03:30] In addition to that, they let it they let the cultures sit for about 24 hours using the butter the butter milk as a starter.
[03:38] So rather than just taking a big vat of milk and scooping off the butter off the top, which is what most most people do with machinery, they actually let that butter sit in butter fat, okay? Which turns into butter milk, and that's how they scrape it. They let it sit there for a whole 24 hours before they do.

[04:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Michelin 3-Star Criteria"

[05:02] When we came back to the table, the entire environment had completely changed. They had different kinds of music. The lighting had changed. It was like a whole different table with a big tablecloth, different centerpieces.
[05:15] They like picked up the flowers in the centerpiece and our bread was like at the bottom of the fuck flower dish for the next meal that we were going to eat. You know, it's weird things that you just don't get at a normal restaurant.

[12:42] There's a random fuck TikTocker who's trying to get some views that went through that whole process of articulating why that butter is worth what it's worth.
[12:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brand Story Control"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[12:58] Brand story is made up of two components to be clear. It's made up of what we say and it's made up of what other people say.
[13:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brand Story = 2 Components"
NÚMEROS: 1. O que NÓS dizemos | 2. O que OUTROS dizem
VISUAL: Equação visual simples, duas colunas lado a lado, ênfase na dualidade do controle da narrativa
[13:12] Think about this very group. Why'd you join in? What'd you hear about? What was that motive?
[13:18] You know, did you know somebody in the group who said, "Hey, this is the shit dude. This is awesome." Did somebody tell you, you know, I joined in? I went from this amount of money to this amount of money.
[13:28] Did you join in just because a few of your friends were a part of it? Did you join in because you like something that I've historically taught you and you thought I could teach you more?
[13:38] Did I control what it was that got you in? Raise your hand if I did.
[13:43] Less than half.
[13:45] So the other half of you, that means that somebody else control the story that you were told about this specific brand that made you buy in. Does that make sense?
[13:55] So once again, brand stories are made up of two sides. What people strategically talk about about our brands and what we talk about and plant about our brands. Does that make sense so far?
[14:05] Okay, to be clear, let me straighten what I'm teaching you.
[14:08] So, to be clear, I want to tie in why this matters, which is the strength of our brand story completely dictates the strength of our pricing.
[14:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brand Story = Pricing Power"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Equação visual: História de Marca Forte → Poder de Precificação, seta indicando relação direta
[14:22] It's the foundation for why we're able to charge what we charge.
[14:26] When I get on the phone with somebody, one of my favorite things to do when I'm on a sales call, it's my favorite question nowadays.
[14:33] Why the fuck you charging 15K and 10% net on a deal? Like, why would I pay you?
[14:40] One of my favorite questions, especially when it's in a niche that I own and dominate, I'm like, "Well, I work with every one of your competitors and I make them all way more money than you're making. So, I think it'd be a good idea for you to just sign on board. It's obviously worth it, huh?"
[14:55] You know, it just shuts it down immediately. You know what I'm saying?
[15:00] The strength in that one sentence, first of all, it's built on truth. It's a fact. But second of all, it's one thing I can say that's a piece of my brand story. One thing.
[15:12] There's hundreds, hundreds of things I could talk about that individually sell people on why I charge what I charge based on what they're buying from me.
[15:22] There's things other people say on behalf of my brands that all it takes typically is one to two sentences that dictates something being worth what it's worth. Does that make sense?
[15:32] Think about the amount of things you bought in your lifetime. You barely heard anything and you're in.
[15:38] Remember the first time you flew first class? You know the first time you flew on a PJ? Oh, it's amazing.
[15:45] You know what' you hear about? What made you buy it?
[15:48] I just got a Rolls-Royce delivered. I I think I've driven in one one time. Why did I just spend that amount of money on a car that I've only driven one time?
[15:58] It's the little things like there's an umbrella in the door, you know? It's the little things like the suspension makes you feel like you're floating. It's the little things.
[16:08] You know what I heard that actually sold me recently? All the buttons are made of metal instead of plastic. I was like, wonder what that's like.
[16:18] It's the fact it's got that little ecstasy on the hood, you know, spirit of ecstasy. I think that's cool.
[16:25] But it's it's not like the whole car like a car salesman selling it to me. There's just little little things like what I just articulated to you, you know, the fact it holds up well through time.
[16:35] The fact, excuse me, the fact that it has the build quality that it does. You know how many build quality stories I've heard about Rolls-Royce for how well it's built and how sturdy and solid and quality it is?
[16:48] Look, did Rolls-Royce has any have any influence in that? Has Rolls-Royce salespeople content ever reached me and made those points?
[16:58] See what I'm saying? It's situations like this. The things I just articulated to you, you might inherit one day as the buying justifications for when you get your Rolls-Royce. That make sense?

[21:15] To be clear, you control your brand story by the content, the results, and the framing that you produce.
[21:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brand Story Control = Content + Results + Framing"
NÚMEROS: 3 componentes principais
VISUAL: Três pilares visuais sustentando "Brand Story", estrutura de fundação
[21:28] Other people control your brand just as much as you do. Does that make sense?
[21:33] We influence people in snap second moments. It's a snap-second judgment.
[21:38] Very few of us have any mental bandwidth to thoroughly research and interpret whether something is quality or not. We gauge it off of others.
[21:48] From an evolution perspective, most of us rely on community of other people who we view as credible for recommendations and for trusted resources.
[21:58] Like when I get up here in front of you as somebody that you trust and I tell you this butter is worth $60, does it substantially increase the probability you go buy it?
[22:08] Yeah.
[22:10] Like those little sentences I tell you like look I make these little snacks sometime in the kitchen. I call them rich snacks as all expensive ingredients you know and it's a I make amazing fuck rich snack.

NÚMEROS: 1 tijolo vs múltiplas pedrinhas
VISUAL: Comparação visual lado a lado: um tijolo grande vs pilha de pequenas pedras
[52:26] What most of us do when our goal is to break a window is unfortunately we pick up a bunch of little fuck pebbles and we throw them at the window and we expect it to crack.
[52:36] We expect it to get that gaping hole that we're looking for.
[52:40] Bricks are heavier than a bunch of little rocks. No, it's more effort. You could throw out your arm pucking a brick at a window.
[52:50] You know, pebbles are technically safer, more efficient, less energy. They don't require as much in order to try to heat it at the window.
[53:00] You could throw a pebble faster than you could throw a brick, right? The effort is reduced. The energy requirement is reduced.
[53:08] What most of us do with our problems is we throw pebbles at them. We just approach what we need to accomplish and we throw pebbles at.
[53:18] Okay. I want you exclusively to be thinking in bricks. Okay? I only want you to be thinking in bricks when it comes to problem solving. Nothing else.
[53:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Think in Bricks, Not Pebbles"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Ênfase visual no tijolo como solução efetiva vs pedrinhas como esforço desperdiçado
[53:32] Does that make sense?
[53:34] Allow me to articulate it with a few scenarios. Okay? I'm going to walk you through five different scenarios with the caveats to each scenario and collectively we are going to attempt to figure out what the brick is.
[53:48] Allow me to summarize what I just said one more time. The brick is the thing that actually solves the fuck problem.
[53:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Brick = Real Solution"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Definição destacada: tijolo = solução real que resolve o problema
[54:00] The pebbles are the things that we think might solve the problem, but are usually less energy intensive, more efficient to do, take less time, and don't break the window.
[54:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pebbles = False Solutions"
NÚMEROS: Características: menos energia, mais eficiente, menos tempo, NÃO resolvem
VISUAL: Lista de características das "pedrinhas", ênfase no fracasso em resolver
[54:18] Give you an example. My clients fuck suck at running their sales teams 95% of the time.
[54:26] My pebbles to solve the problem that were more energy-efficient for me. I am efficient for me were I created an entire sales training program for them.
[54:36] Specific with every fuck problem they're all dealing with. You think they watched it and that made the difference?
[54:44] Fuck no.
[54:46] Hey, okay. I must be a bad sales trainer then. I'm going to refer them as many sales trainers as I can that they need to buy that training from and put their people through.
[54:58] You think that did it? Pebble didn't break the window.
[55:02] I then was like, "Fuck, dude. It's got to be the sales manager." Uh I like most of them don't even have sales managers.
[55:12] I'm going to go and train the business owner on what they specifically need to do. I'm going to take all my best practices that I'm aware of from doing this tens of thousands of fuck times and I'm going to see if I can train the business owner to do it.
[55:26] You think they give a fuck to actually do it? You'd think they would. That's what I thought. They didn't. Never. More waste of time.
[55:36] eventually just became so frustrating. Why is the window breaking? I'm fuck dinking pebbles off of it.
[55:44] The brick, you know what the brick was, which I knew the whole time I needed to do, but I didn't want to do it because it takes too much energy, too much, too much effort.
[55:56] Okay, you know what it was? It was taking over the sales teams completely. That's what did it.
[56:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Brick Solution: Complete Sales Team Takeover"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Progressão de tentativas falhadas (pedrinhas) vs solução final (tijolo): assumir completamente o time de vendas
[56:10] taking over the fuck sales team, running the fuck sales team, recruiting the sales people, training the sales people, literally doing all of that's what made the difference.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Animal Farm Butter: $60 por unidade
Presidente Butter: $1.99
Kerrygold Butter: $6-7
Jersey Milk: +18% proteína, +20% cálcio, +25% gordura
Michelin 3 estrelas: <12 nos EUA, <30 no mundo
Frost NYC Watch: $1,500
Richard Mille preço médio: $250,000-$300,000
Richard Mille entry-level: $130,000
Richard Mille premium: $1,500,000 (30 unidades produzidas)
Landing page case study: $40,000 investidos, ROAS 8:1
Evolução de preço: $5,000 → $60,000 + percentual
Cliente médio: $100,000/mês
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Brand Story = 2 componentes: o que você diz + o que outros dizem
Snap-second judgments: decisões instantâneas baseadas em percepção visual
Content control através de: conteúdo + resultados + framing
Justificações de compra são construídas antes e depois da conversa de vendas
Análise de perfil Instagram para avaliar seriedade profissional
Evitar travel pics excessivos para manter percepção de foco
Criar "rich snacks" e lifestyle content que demonstra consistência
Usar case studies visuais (landing pages, utility rooms, etc.)
Leverage de relacionamentos (Rico → Cody, testimonials indiretos)
"Think in Bricks, Not Pebbles" para resolução de problemas
ESTRUTURA:
Foundation: Brand Story → Pricing Power
Percepção controla precificação
Escassez + Exclusividade = Valor Premium
Michelin System: 1⭐ comida | 2⭐⭐ comida+serviço | 3⭐⭐⭐ comida+serviço+experiência
French Laundry adquire Animal Farm para fornecimento exclusivo
Contractor example: visual profissional + expertise técnico = percepção de alto valor
Pebbles (soluções superficiais) vs Brick (solução real que resolve)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Margem butter: $1.99 → $6-7 (3.5x) → $60 (30x)
Por unidade Animal Farm: $15 por bola ($60÷4)
ROAS demonstrado: 8:1 ($40K → $320K em 30 dias)
Crescimento de pricing: 12x ($5K → $60K)
Warehouse client: 800,000 sq ft, $4M/mês
Rico: primeiro milhão/mês alcançado
DURAÇÃO: ~56 minutos FORMATO: Apresentação ao vivo para grupo/mastermind, estilo workshop interativo com exercícios práticos
